_comment: Human curation layer. Merged OVER the API scaffold at build time; a refresh never overwrites these fields. Each entry is keyed by {congress}-{type}-{number}. 'summary' and 'provisions' are your own words (summarySource=human). 'significance' and 'angle' are preserved from the original tool but are NOT rendered under the current design; delete them or flip SHOW_SIGNIFICANCE in config.py if you want them back. pinned=true keeps these on the docket regardless of the salience filter.

bills.title: Digital Asset Market CLARITY Act of 2025
bills.sponsor: Rep. French Hill (R-AR) & Rep. G.T. Thompson (R-PA)
bills.summary: Establishes the framework dividing digital-asset oversight between the SEC and CFTC, with the CFTC taking the lead on 'digital commodities'; folds in the Anti-CBDC Surveillance State Act.
provisions: Defines a 'digital commodity' as an asset whose value is intrinsically linked to use of a blockchain; excludes securities, derivatives, and stablecoins.
Gives the CFTC central authority over digital-commodity spot markets and intermediaries; preserves limited SEC authority over primary-market fundraising via a tailored registration exemption.
Creates expedited / provisional registration pathways for brokers, dealers, and exchanges.
Bars Federal Reserve banks from offering products directly to individuals and prohibits a CBDC for monetary policy (Anti-CBDC title).
bills.significance: The House-passed vehicle that both Senate committees are now building on. Passage requires reconciling two Senate drafts (Banking and Agriculture) and then a House-Senate conference, a heavy lift before the November 2026 midterms.
bills.angle: State money-transmitter and trust regimes sit in the background of any market-structure deal: how 'digital commodity' intermediaries are licensed federally will shape what's left for state supervision.

bills.title: Digital Commodity Intermediaries Act
bills.sponsor: Sen. John Boozman (R-AR), Chairman, Senate Agriculture
bills.status: Reported by Senate Ag (party-line); on calendar
bills.summary: The Senate Agriculture Committee's CFTC-side companion to the CLARITY Act, focused on who must register as a digital-commodity intermediary and how spot-market trading is supervised.
provisions: Provides a statutory definition of 'digital commodities' and a CFTC spot-market intermediary regime.
Customer-fund segregation, conflict-of-interest safeguards, and customer-disclosure requirements.
A trading-registration regime intended to onshore liquid, regulated markets while protecting retail participants.
Directs CFTC-SEC coordination on interagency rulemakings; carve-outs for software developers.
bills.significance: Advanced on a party-line vote Jan 29, 2026 and reported Feb 2. Must be reconciled with the separate Senate Banking (SEC-side) draft, then with the House CLARITY Act, the bottleneck in the crypto market-structure effort.
bills.angle: By design it leaves DeFi-protocol treatment open and works the CFTC side; the SEC/securities boundary is being fought out in Banking.

bills.title: 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act
bills.sponsor: Sen. Tim Scott (R-SC) & Sen. Elizabeth Warren (D-MA)
bills.status: Passed both chambers (differing texts); reconciliation pending
bills.summary: The marquee bipartisan housing package, combining the Senate's ROAD to Housing Act (S. 2651) and the House's Housing for the 21st Century Act, to boost supply, modernize HUD/FHA programs, and curb institutional ownership of single-family homes.
provisions: Increases FHA multifamily loan limits and modernizes HOME, CDBG, and related HUD programs.
Includes a community-banking title strengthening banks' role in the housing market.
Restricts large institutional investors from amassing single-family homes (responding to E.O. 14376).
Streamlines environmental review and rethinks rules that hamper small-dollar mortgage lending.
bills.significance: The furthest-along bill on this docket. The Senate passed H.R. 6644 with substitute amendment (89-10) on Mar 12, 2026; the House passed an amended version in May. The two chambers are reconciling; the White House has signaled it would sign.
bills.angle: The community-bank housing title and small-dollar-mortgage provisions are where this otherwise housing-focused package intersects with bank supervision.

bills.title: SMART Act of 2025
bills.summary: Supervisory Modifications for Appropriate Risk-based Testing, alternating limited-scope exams for well-managed, well-capitalized institutions of $6 billion or less.
provisions: Amends FDI Act §10(d): after a full-scope, on-site exam, the next exam of a qualifying IDI is a limited-scope exam.
Applies to well-managed, well-capitalized IDIs and credit unions with ≤$6B in consolidated assets.
Lets institutions request consolidation of separate safety-and-soundness, consumer-compliance, and IT/cyber exams.
Directs regulators to issue implementing rules within 12 months; preserves authority for targeted reviews.
bills.significance: Passed the House and was received in the Senate (read twice, referred to Banking) on May 13, 2026, one of the more advanced supervisory-relief measures.
bills.angle: Exam-cycle relief shapes state-federal alternating exam programs; the NCUA already holds comparable authority, a point the credit-union trades press.

bills.title: TRUST Act of 2025
bills.sponsor: Rep. Tim Moore (R-NC) & Rep. Ritchie Torres (D-NY)
bills.status: Reported by Committee; on Union Calendar No. 209
bills.summary: Tailored Regulatory Updates for Supervisory Testing, lengthens the exam cycle by raising the asset threshold for the 18-month examination schedule.
provisions: Raises the consolidated-asset threshold to qualify for an 18-month exam cycle from $3 billion to $6 billion.
Applies to insured depository institutions that are well-capitalized and well-managed at their most recent exam.
Frames the change as completing the tailoring intended (but, sponsors argue, under-implemented) since 2018.
bills.significance: Reported favorably (H. Rept. 119-252) after a 48-0 markup and placed on the Union Calendar; a Senate companion (S. 3830) has been referred to Banking.
bills.angle: Longer cycles for healthy community banks free supervisory resources but raise the perennial state-federal question of who carries the alternating exam.

bills.title: Community Bank Regulatory Tailoring Act
bills.sponsor: Rep. Andy Barr (R-KY) & Rep. Josh Gottheimer (D-NJ)
bills.status: Reported by Committee; on Union Calendar No. 480
bills.summary: Indexes a slate of asset-based statutory thresholds for bank regulation to nominal GDP, so community-bank trigger points keep pace with economic growth.
provisions: Indexes asset-based regulatory thresholds for community banks and small credit unions to nominal GDP.
Raises Bank Holding Company Act thresholds (e.g., $1M->$3M; $10B->$15B for certain provisions).
Raises a Community Reinvestment Act threshold (e.g., $250M->$800M).
bills.significance: Reported (H. Rept. 119-558) after a 33-21 markup. A non-germane Waters amendment to add a 10% credit-card rate cap was withdrawn.
bills.angle: GDP-indexing is the structural cousin of the Main Street omnibus's 'Tailoring and Indexing' title; it ends the slow drift of fixed-dollar thresholds into ever-smaller banks.

bills.title: Promoting New Bank Formation Act
bills.status: Reported by Committee; on Union Calendar No. 64
bills.summary: Eases the path for de novo banks, a 3-year capital phase-in, relief for rural de novos, and faster review of business-plan changes, to reverse the collapse in new-bank charters.
provisions: Directs the banking agencies to establish a 3-year phase-in for de novo institutions to meet federal capital standards.
Provides targeted relief for de novo rural community banks.
Sets a 30-day clock on business-plan-change requests during the phase-in, with automatic approval if the agency misses it.
Requires a joint agency study on the causes of low de novo formation.
bills.significance: Reported (H. Rept. 119-90, 28-21) and also carried as Section 101 of the Main Street Capital Access Act. A Senate companion (S. 113, Hyde-Smith) sits in Banking.
bills.angle: De novo formation is a structural concern for state regulators: most new charters historically begin as state banks, so a federal capital on-ramp directly affects the state pipeline.

bills.title: Least Cost Exception Act
bills.sponsor: Rep. Mike Flood (R-NE) & Rep. Bill Foster (D-IL)
bills.status: Reported by Committee; on Union Calendar No. 405
bills.summary: Lets the FDIC waive the least-cost-resolution mandate to pursue alternative resolutions of failed banks, specifically to avoid pushing failed banks into the largest G-SIBs.
provisions: Amends the FDI Act to create an exception to the least-cost-resolution (LCR) requirement.
Favors resolution alternatives that do not further concentrate assets in global systemically important banks (G-SIBs).
Aims to give the FDIC flexibility to weigh competitive and economic effects beyond the narrow cost to the Deposit Insurance Fund.
bills.significance: Reported (H. Rept. 119-474) after a 50-0 markup and carried as Section 701 of the Main Street omnibus.
bills.angle: A direct answer to the 2023 regional-bank failures: a rigid LCR mandate can entrench concentration. State regulators care because their mid-size charters are the most likely 'alternative' acquirers.

bills.title: Failing Bank Acquisition Fairness Act
bills.status: Reported by Committee; on Union Calendar No. 406
bills.summary: The transparency counterpart to merger-relief bills: conditions any waiver of merger concentration limits in failing-bank deals and forces detailed reporting to Congress.
provisions: Permits an exception to deposit-concentration limits only where a bank is in default or in danger of default and non-approval would cause significant disruption or harm financial stability.
Requires a detailed report to Congress justifying any waiver of the limits.
Requires disclosure of alternative bids considered and the reasons each was rejected.
bills.significance: Reported (H. Rept. 119-475), the Democratic-led accountability bookend to the FDIC-flexibility push in the Least Cost Exception Act.
bills.angle: Pairs naturally with the Least Cost Exception Act: one widens FDIC discretion in failures, the other demands transparency about how that discretion (and concentration waivers) is used.

bills.title: Keeping Deposits Local Act
bills.sponsor: Rep. Tom Emmer (R-MN) & Rep. Joyce Beatty (D-OH)
bills.summary: Expands how much in reciprocal deposits a bank can hold before they count as 'brokered,' replacing the flat cap with a tiered, liability-based schedule.
provisions: Amends FDI Act §29(i): replaces the lesser-of-20%-or-$5B reciprocal-deposit cap with tiers scaled to total liabilities.
Lets institutions rated CAMELS 1, 2, or 3 qualify (vs. the current 'outstanding or good' composite standard).
Builds on Emmer's 2018 reciprocal-deposit reform.
bills.significance: Passed the House 405-0 on May 20, 2026 and was referred to Senate Banking, among the broadest bipartisan margins on the docket. Reciprocal balances have grown from ~$156B (2022) to >$422B (2025).
bills.angle: Brokered-deposit classification drives capital and rate restrictions; expanding the reciprocal carve-out keeps large municipal and corporate relationships at community banks rather than the megabanks.

bills.title: Community Bank Deposit Access Act
bills.summary: The custodial-deposit companion to Keeping Deposits Local, lets community banks hold custodial deposits without the brokered label, subject to a liability-based cap.
provisions: Reclassifies qualifying custodial deposits so they are not treated as brokered deposits.
Caps the carve-out at custodial deposits not exceeding 20% of a bank's total liabilities.
Targets the same goal as the reciprocal-deposit reform: stable funding for local lending.
bills.significance: Passed the House 393-16 alongside the housing package in May 2026; now before the Senate. Together with H.R. 3234 it forms the House's two-part rewrite of brokered-deposit treatment.
bills.angle: The brokered-deposit framework is a recurring flashpoint between the FDIC's funding-stability concerns and community-bank liquidity needs, squarely a safety-and-soundness question state examiners share.

bills.title: Small LENDER Act
bills.summary: Narrows the reach of the CFPB's §1071 small-business-lending data rule, Small Lenders Exempt from New Data and Excessive Reporting, via exemptions, a safe harbor, and a later effective date.
provisions: Amends ECOA §704B (15 U.S.C. 1691c-2) to exempt lenders below a high origination threshold (reported at fewer than 2,500 small-business loans).
Limits 'small business' to those with $1M or less in annual revenue.
Deletes certain data points (e.g., pricing, denial reasons) and adds a temporary compliance safe harbor and delayed effective date.
bills.significance: Reported out of committee 26-22 in April 2026. It is the 'mitigate' option alongside the more aggressive H.R. 976 (full §1071 repeal); the CFPB's May 2026 final rule already pushed compliance to Jan 1, 2028.
bills.angle: A core dual-banking asymmetry: §1071 reaches bank and non-bank lenders unevenly. Defining who reports, and on what, reshapes the fair-lending data picture state regulators rely on.

bills.title: 1071 Repeal to Protect Small Business Lending Act
bills.summary: The maximalist counterpart to the Small LENDER Act, repeals the §1071 small-business-lending data-collection statute outright.
provisions: Repeals the small-business-loan data-collection requirements added to ECOA by Dodd-Frank §1071.
Eliminates mandatory collection/reporting of application disposition, loan type/amount, and applicant census tract, revenue, race, sex, and ethnicity.
bills.significance: Reported by the House Financial Services Committee in April 2025. Where the Small LENDER Act narrows §1071, this bill removes its statutory foundation.
bills.angle: Repealing the statute (rather than trimming the rule) would moot the entire CFPB rulemaking cycle, a cleaner outcome for lenders but a loss of the fair-lending dataset advocates defend.

bills.title: American Lending Fairness Act of 2026
bills.sponsor: Sen. Bernie Moreno (R-OH); House companion Rep. Warren Davidson (R-OH)
bills.summary: Restores nationwide interest-rate exportation parity for state-chartered banks by limiting a state's DIDMCA opt-out to loans made by its own chartered institutions.
provisions: Amends FDI Act §27 (12 U.S.C. 1831d) and the Federal Credit Union Act on rate exportation.
Provides that a state's opt-out from federal rate preemption applies only to loans made by institutions that state chartered, not to out-of-state state banks/credit unions.
Repeals §525 of DIDMCA (1980) and addresses the legal effect of prior state opt-outs.
bills.significance: A direct response to the Tenth Circuit's 2025 decision in National Association of Industrial Bankers v. Weiser, which let Colorado apply its caps to out-of-state lenders. Backed by bank trades as preserving dual-banking 'competitive parity.'
bills.angle: This is the preemption-restoring pole of the rate debate, and the one most explicitly framed around the dual banking system. It is the mirror image of S. 3721.

bills.title: Empowering States' Rights To Protect Consumers Act of 2026
bills.sponsor: Sen. Sheldon Whitehouse (D-RI), with Warren, Reed & Merkley
bills.summary: The consumer-side pole of the rate-preemption fight: amends the Truth in Lending Act to let states set the maximum APR on consumer-credit transactions in their borders.
provisions: Amends TILA to empower states to set maximum annual percentage rates on consumer credit transactions.
Aims to close the 'bank exemption' that lets out-of-state state banks export rates above local caps.
Targets so-called rent-a-bank arrangements used to exceed state usury limits.
bills.significance: Reintroduces a Whitehouse proposal from prior Congresses. Consumer groups support it and oppose S. 3889; industry takes the reverse view, the two bills define the poles of the 2026 rate-exportation debate.
bills.angle: Where S. 3889 restores federal preemption parity, S. 3721 expands state authority. For a state-regulator audience, the pair is the cleanest live illustration of the federalism tension in consumer credit.

bills.title: PACE Act of 2026
bills.sponsor: Rep. Young Kim (R-CA) & Rep. Sam Liccardo (D-CA)
bills.summary: Payments Access and Consumer Efficiency Act, creates an optional federal 'registered covered provider' status at the OCC for nonbank payment firms, with a pathway to direct Federal Reserve payment-rail access.
provisions: Establishes an optional OCC-administered federal supervisory framework for qualifying nonbank payment providers.
Lets a registered provider holding 40+ state money-transmitter licenses operate nationwide; provides a pathway to apply for a Fed payments reserve account (Fedwire, FedNow, FedACH).
Requires 1:1 reserves, risk-management, recordkeeping, and consumer-protection standards.
Adds an insolvency framework prioritizing customer payment obligations over general creditors.
bills.significance: Backed by major fintech and digital-asset trades. Notably, the draft contains no express preemption clause, leaving the relationship between 'nationwide operation' and the state money-transmitter regime unresolved. No Senate companion yet.
bills.angle: This is the bill on the docket most directly in tension with state authority: an OCC federal registration that confers nationwide reach would sit atop, and arguably around, the state MTL system that currently licenses these firms.

bills.title: SECURE Data Act
bills.sponsor: Rep. John Joyce (R-PA), Vice Chair, Energy & Commerce
bills.status: Introduced; referred to E&C and Judiciary
bills.summary: The first comprehensive national consumer-privacy framework of the 119th Congress, establishing data rights enforced by the FTC and state AGs, with federal preemption of state privacy law.
provisions: Creates consumer rights to access, correct, delete, and port personal data and to opt out of targeted advertising, sales, and consequential profiling.
FTC and state AGs share enforcement; a 45-day cure period precedes formal action; no private right of action.
Exempts financial institutions and data covered by the Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act, instead covered by the companion GUARD Act.
Exempts HIPAA-covered entities, nonprofits, and higher-education institutions to avoid duplicating sector rules.
related: H.R. 8398 (GUARD Financial Data Act, the GLBA half)
bills.significance: Product of the House E&C Data Privacy Working Group; supported by 50+ trade associations but given long odds in its current form. Its GLBA carve-out is the seam where it hands financial data to the GUARD Act.
bills.angle: Preemption of state privacy law is the headline fight. The deliberate GLBA carve-out is why this and the GUARD Act must be read as a single, two-committee package.

bills.title: GUARD Financial Data Act
bills.sponsor: Rep. Bill Huizenga (R-MI), with Hill & Steil
bills.summary: Guidelines for Use, Access, and Responsible Disclosure of Financial Data, modernizes Title V of the Gramm-Leach-Bliley Act as the financial-sector half of the privacy package.
provisions: Adds a data-minimization duty: collection/disclosure of nonpublic personal information limited to what is adequate, relevant, and reasonably necessary.
Provides a continuing consumer opt-out right and limits on use of consumer access credentials.
Requires affirmative opt-in consent before sensitive personal information is disclosed.
Preempts state laws in the financial-activity space; directs consideration of effects on small institutions (approx. <=$15M).
related: H.R. 8413 (SECURE Data Act, the non-GLBA half)
bills.significance: Introduced in tandem with the SECURE Data Act so banks follow one national standard rather than a state-plus-federal patchwork. Observers call its preemption language about as strong as a federal privacy bill is likely to get.
bills.angle: GLBA modernization plus broad financial-activity preemption directly touches state financial-privacy and data-security regimes, a coordination question for state regulators, not just a compliance update.

bills.title: Main Street Capital Access Act
bills.sponsor: Rep. French Hill (R-AR) & Rep. Andy Barr (R-KY)
bills.summary: The omnibus vehicle. It bundles many of the stand-alone community-bank bills on this docket into one eight-title package on de novo formation, tailoring, supervision, deposits, resolution, and fintech.
provisions: Title I - New bank formation & local access: Promoting New Bank Formation (§101), Bank Failure Prevention (§103).
Title II - Tailoring: TAILOR Act (§201), small-BHC relief (§202), CBLR flexibility (§203), Tailoring & Indexing (§204).
Title III - Supervision: HUMPS (§301), FAIR Exams (§302), SMART (§303), TRUST (§304), stress-test transparency (§305).
Title VII - Resolutions: Least Cost Exception (§701). Title VIII - Innovation: Merchant Banking Modernization (§801), Bank-Fintech Partnership (§802).
bills.significance: Because several bills here also ride inside this omnibus, the omnibus's fate can carry, or substitute for, its components. Watch whether leadership advances the package or the stand-alones.
bills.angle: For tracking purposes: a stand-alone bill 'stalling' may simply mean it has migrated into this vehicle. Always check the omnibus before concluding a component is dead.
